content:
You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Maintainers provided a patch, but patches often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original patch description and related test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the patch)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

135 entry:
136   %search_test = icmp eq ptr %search_start, %search_end
137   %needle_test = icmp eq ptr %needle_start, %needle_end
138   %combined_test = or i1 %search_test, %needle_test
139   br i1 %combined_test, label %exit, label %header
140 
141 header:
142   %search_ptr = phi ptr [ %search_next, %search_check ], [ %search_start, %entry ]
143   %search_load = load i8, ptr %search_ptr, align 1
144   br label %match_check
145 
146 needle_check:
147   %needle_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %needle_ptr, i64 1
148   %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
149   br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check
150 
151 match_check:
152   %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
153   %needle_load = load i8, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
154   %match_cmp = icmp eq i8 %search_load, %needle_load
155   br i1 %match_cmp, label %exit, label %needle_check
156 
157 search_check:
158   %search_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
159   %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
160   br i1 %search_cmp, label %exit, label %header
161 
162 exit:
163   %res = phi ptr [ %search_end, %entry ], [ %search_ptr, %match_check ], [ %search_end, %search_check ]
164   ret ptr %res
165 }

292   %search_test = icmp eq ptr %search_start, %search_end
293   %needle_test = icmp eq ptr %needle_start, %needle_end
294   %combined_test = or i1 %search_test, %needle_test
295   br i1 %combined_test, label %exit, label %header

297 header:
298   %search_ptr = phi ptr [ %search_next, %search_check ], [ %search_start, %entry ]
299   %search_load = load i16, ptr %search_ptr, align 1
300   br label %match_check
301 
302 needle_check:
303   %needle_next = getelementptr inbounds i16, ptr %needle_ptr, i64 1
304   %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
305   br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check
306 
307 match_check:
308   %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
309   %needle_load = load i16, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
310   %match_cmp = icmp eq i16 %search_load, %needle_load
311   br i1 %match_cmp, label %exit, label %needle_check
312 
313 search_check:
314   %search_next = getelementptr inbounds i16, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
315   %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
316   br i1 %search_cmp, label %exit, label %header
317 
318 exit:
319   %res = phi ptr [ %search_end, %entry ], [ %search_ptr, %match_check ], [ %search_end, %search_check ]
320   ret ptr %res
321 }

453 entry:

455   %needle_test = icmp eq ptr %needle_start, %needle_end

459 header:
460   %search_ptr = phi ptr [ %search_next, %search_check ], [ %search_start, %entry ]
461   %search_load = load i8, ptr %search_ptr, align 1
462   br label %match_check
463 
464 needle_check:
465   %needle_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %needle_ptr, i64 1
466   %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
467   br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check
468 
469 match_check:
470   %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
471   %needle_load = load i8, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
472   %match_cmp = icmp eq i8 %search_load, %needle_load
473   br i1 %match_cmp, label %exit_succ, label %needle_check
474 
475 search_check:
476   %search_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
477   %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
478   br i1 %search_cmp, label %exit_fail, label %header
479 
480 exit_succ:
481   %res_succ = phi ptr [ %search_ptr, %match_check ]
482   br label %exit
483 
484 exit_fail:
485   %res_fail = phi ptr [ %search_end, %entry ], [ %search_end, %search_check ]
486   br label %exit
487 
488 exit:
489   %res = phi ptr [ %res_succ, %exit_succ ], [ %res_fail, %exit_fail ]
490   ret ptr %res

893   %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
894   br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check
895 
896 match_check:
897   %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
898   %needle_load = load i8, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
899   %match_cmp = icmp eq i8 %search_load, %needle_load
900   br i1 %match_cmp, label %exit, label %needle_check
901 
902 search_check:
903   %search_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
904   %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
905   br i1 %search_cmp, label %exit, label %header
906 
907 exit:
908   %res = phi ptr [ %search_end, %entry ], [ %search_ptr, %match_check ], [ %search_end, %search_check ]
909   %use = phi ptr [ %needle_end, %entry ], [ %needle_ptr, %match_check ], [ %needle_end, %search_check ]
910   ret ptr %res
911 }
912

914 ; which should block the transform.
915 define ptr @find_first_of_i8_multi_exit_outside_use(ptr %search_start, ptr %search_end, ptr %needle_start, ptr %needle_end) #0 {
916 ; CHECK-LABEL: define ptr @find_first_of_i8_multi_exit_outside_use(
917 ; CHECK-NOT: {{%.*}} @llvm.experimental.vector.match{{.*}}
918 ;
919 ; DISABLE-LABEL: define ptr @find_first_of_i8_multi_exit_outside_use(
920 ; DISABLE-NOT: {{%.*}} @llvm.experimental.vector.match{{.*}}
921 ;
922 entry:
923   %search_test = icmp eq ptr %search_start, %search_end
924   %needle_test = icmp eq ptr %needle_start, %needle_end
925   %combined_test = or i1 %search_test, %needle_test
926   br i1 %combined_test, label %exit_fail, label %header
927 
928 header:
929   %search_ptr = phi ptr [ %search_next, %search_check ], [ %search_start, %entry ]
930   %search_load = load i8, ptr %search_ptr, align 1
931   br label %match_check
932 
933 needle_check:
934   %needle_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %needle_ptr, i64 1
935   %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
936   br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check
937 
938 match_check:
939   %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
940   %needle_load = load i8, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
941   %match_cmp = icmp eq i8 %search_load, %needle_load
942   br i1 %match_cmp, label %exit_succ, label %needle_check
943 
944 search_check:
945   %search_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
946   %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
947   br i1 %search_cmp, label %exit_fail, label %header
948 
949 exit_succ:
950   %res_succ = phi ptr [ %search_ptr, %match_check ]
951   br label %exit
952 
953 exit_fail:
954   %res_fail = phi ptr [ %search_end, %entry ], [ %search_ptr, %search_check ]
955   br label %exit
956 
957 exit:
958   %res = phi ptr [ %res_succ, %exit_succ ], [ %res_fail, %exit_fail ]
959   ret ptr %res
960 }

output: Error: 'experimental.vector.match' is not found in the LLVM Language Reference Manual.

name: stop
arguments:
{"thoughts":"Fix Understanding:
The patch addresses an issue in `LoopIdiomVectorize` for the `find_first_of` idiom.
Previously, the inner loop (iterating over the needle array) would early-exit as soon as ANY match was found between the current search vector and the current needle vector.
However, this is incorrect because a subsequent needle vector might match an EARLIER element in the search vector.
For example, if search vector is `[X, Y, A, B]` and needle is `[B, A]`.
In the first inner loop iteration, needle vector `[B, ...]` is compared. It matches `B` at index 3. The old code would early exit and say the first match is at index 3.
But in the second inner loop iteration, needle vector `[A, ...]` would match `A` at index 2, which is earlier!
The patch fixes this by accumulating the match predicates (`MatchAcc = Match | MatchSeg`) across all needle vectors for the current search vector.
Only after the inner loop finishes does it check if any match was found (`IfAnyMatch = OrReduce(MatchAcc)`), and if so, it uses `cttz` on the accumulated match predicate to find the earliest match in the search vector.

Formal Semantic Modeling:
Before fix:
For each search vector S:
  For each needle vector N:
    M = match(S, N)
    if (any(M)) return cttz(M)

After fix:
For each search vector S:
  M_acc = 0
  For each needle vector N:
    M = match(S, N)
    M_acc = M_acc | M
  if (any(M_acc)) return cttz(M_acc)

Assumptions Identified:
1. The match intrinsic `experimental_vector_match` returns a boolean vector where each element `i` is true if `S[i]` matches ANY element in `N`.
2. `cttz` on the accumulated boolean vector correctly identifies the lowest index `i` where `S[i]` matched any element in the entire needle array.
3. The inactive lanes of the needle vector are filled with `Needle[0]`. This is safe because `Needle[0]` is a valid needle element, so matching it doesn't introduce false positives (it just redundantly finds matches for `Needle[0]`).
4. The inactive lanes of the search vector are masked out in the `match` intrinsic, so they don't produce matches.

Potential Cases to Break Assumptions:
1. What if `Needle[0]` is used to fill inactive lanes, but we are in the last iteration of the needle loop, and the active lanes don't match, but the inactive lanes (filled with `Needle[0]`) DO match?
Wait, if `Needle[0]` matches a search element, that search element is a valid match! It doesn't matter if it was found via an inactive lane or an active lane. The only issue would be if `Needle[0]` was NOT part of the needle array, but it is.
2. What if the search array has a match in an inactive lane? The `match` intrinsic takes `PredSearch` as an argument, which masks out inactive search lanes. So this should be safe.
3. Is there any issue with LCSSA or PHI nodes? The patch updates the PHI nodes correctly. `Match` accumulates in `BB2`. `MatchPredAccLCSSA` gets it in `BB3`. `MatchPredLCSSA` gets it in `BB4`.
4. What if the needle array is larger than the search array? The loops handle this correctly.

Test Strategies:
1. A test where the first needle vector matches a later search element, and the second needle vector matches an earlier search element. This directly tests the bug the patch fixes.
2. A test where the match is found in the last needle vector, and it matches the first search element.
3. A test where the needle array length is not a multiple of VF, so the last needle vector has inactive lanes filled with `Needle[0]`. Ensure this doesn't cause incorrect behavior.
4. A test where the search array length is not a multiple of VF, so the last search vector has inactive lanes. Ensure matches in inactive search lanes are ignored.
5. A test with multiple exits or outside uses to ensure the PHI node updates don't break LCSSA or dominance.","strategies":[{"name":"Needle Splatting Inactive Lanes","target":"Needle array loading and splatting","expected_issue":"The vector match intrinsic might return a match for an element that is out of bounds of the search array, because the search array is masked but the match intrinsic might not respect the mask correctly, or the mask is not applied to the match result. Wait, the match intrinsic takes the search mask as an argument. But what if the needle array has out-of-bounds elements? The needle array is masked, but the inactive lanes are filled with the first element of the needle array. If the first element of the needle array matches an element in the search array, it will register a match for the inactive lanes of the needle array! Wait, the match intrinsic checks if ANY element in the needle array matches the search element. If we splat the first element of the needle array to the inactive lanes, and that element matches a search element, it will be considered a match, even if the first element was already processed in a previous iteration or is currently being processed. This is actually correct because if the first element matches, it's a valid match. But what if the needle array is empty? The loop is guarded by a check that the needle array is not empty.","rationale":"The needle array is loaded with a mask, and inactive lanes are filled with the first element of the needle array. If the first element matches an element in the search array, it will register a match. If a search element matches a needle element in the first needle vector, and another search element matches a needle element in the second needle vector, both matches will be recorded in `MatchAcc`. `cttz` will then find the lowest index in `MatchAcc`, which corresponds to the earliest search element that matched ANY needle element. This is correct. But what if the same search element matches multiple needle elements? It will just be ORed together, which is fine. What if the loop over the needle array is executed multiple times? The `Match` PHI node accumulates the matches. Is it possible that `MatchAcc` accumulates matches from previous search vectors? No, `Match` is initialized to 0 for each new search vector in `BB1`."
    ],
    [
      "Missed Early Exit Optimization",
      "Inner loop termination condition",
      "The inner loop always runs to completion, even if a match is found at the first element of the search vector. This is a missed optimization.",
      "The fix changes the control flow so that the inner loop (needle array) always runs to completion before checking for a match. This means that even if a match is found early in the needle array, the loop will continue to process the rest of the needle array. This could lead to performance degradation, but it is necessary for correctness to find the earliest match in the search array. However, what if the needle array is very large? The inner loop will execute many times. Is there a way to early exit if we have found a match at the very first element of the search vector? If `MatchAcc` has the lowest bit set, we know the first element of the search vector matched, and we can't possibly find an earlier match. The current implementation doesn't do this early exit, which is a missed optimization but not a correctness bug."
    ],
    [
      "Empty Array Handling",
      "Preheader checks for empty arrays",
      "The code assumes the search and needle arrays are not empty. We should verify that the preheader checks correctly handle empty arrays.",
      "The `MatchPredAccLCSSA` PHI node in `BB3` receives `MatchAcc` from `BB2`. `BB3` then checks if any match was found using `OrReduce`. If a match is found, it branches to `BB4` to calculate the index. `BB4` uses `MatchPredLCSSA` which receives `MatchPredAccLCSSA` from `BB3`. This seems correct. But what if the search array is empty? The loop is guarded by a check that the search array is not empty. What if the needle array is empty? The loop is guarded by a check that the needle array is not empty."
    ]
  ],
  "thoughts": "Fix Understanding:
The patch addresses an issue in `LoopIdiomVectorize` for the `find_first_of` idiom.
Previously, the inner loop (iterating over the needle array) would early-exit as soon as ANY match was found between the current search vector and the current needle vector.
However, this is incorrect because a subsequent needle vector might match an EARLIER element in the search vector.
For example, if search vector is `[X, Y, A, B]` and needle is `[B, A]`.
In the first inner loop iteration, needle vector `[B, ...]` is compared. It matches `B` at index 3. The old code would early exit and say the first match is at index 3.
But in the second inner loop iteration, needle vector `[A, ...]` would match `A` at index 2, which is earlier!
The patch fixes this by accumulating the match predicates (`MatchAcc = Match | MatchSeg`) across all needle vectors for the current search vector.
Only after the inner loop finishes does it check if any match was found (`IfAnyMatch = OrReduce(MatchAcc)`), and if so, it uses `cttz` on the accumulated match predicate to find the earliest match in the search vector.

Formal Semantic Modeling:
Before fix:
For each search vector S:
  For each needle vector N:
    M = match(S, N)
    if (any(M)) return cttz(M)

After fix:
For each search vector S:
  M_acc = 0
  For each needle vector N:
    M = match(S, N)
    M_acc = M_acc | M
  if (any(M_acc)) return cttz(M_acc)

Assumptions Identified:
1. The match intrinsic `experimental_vector_match` returns a boolean vector where each element `i` is true if `S[i]` matches ANY element in `N`.
2. `cttz` on the accumulated boolean vector correctly identifies the lowest index `i` where `S[i]` matched any element in the entire needle array.
3. The inactive lanes of the needle vector are filled with `Needle[0]`. This is safe because `Needle[0]` is a valid needle element, so matching it doesn't introduce false positives (it just redundantly finds matches for `Needle[0]`).
4. The inactive lanes of the search vector are masked out in the `match` intrinsic, so they don't produce matches.

Potential Cases to Break Assumptions:
1. What if `Needle[0]` is used to fill inactive lanes, but we are in the last iteration of the needle loop, and the active lanes don't match, but the inactive lanes (filled with `Needle[0]`) DO match?
Wait, if `Needle[0]` matches a search element, that search element is a valid match! It doesn't matter if it was found via an inactive lane or an active lane. The only issue would be if `Needle[0]` was NOT part of the needle array, but it is.
2. What if the search array has a match in an inactive lane? The `match` intrinsic takes `PredSearch` as an argument, which masks out inactive search lanes. So this should be safe.
3. Is there any issue with LCSSA or PHI nodes? The patch updates the PHI nodes correctly. `Match` accumulates in `BB2`. `MatchPredAccLCSSA` gets it in `BB3`. `MatchPredLCSSA` gets it in `BB4`.
4. What if the needle array is larger than the search array? The loops handle this correctly.

Test Strategies:
1. A test where the first needle vector matches a later search element, and the second needle vector matches an earlier search element. This directly tests the bug the patch fixes.
2. A test where the match is found in the last needle vector, and it matches the first search element.
3. A test where the needle array length is not a multiple of VF, so the last needle vector has inactive lanes filled with `Needle[0]`. Ensure this doesn't cause incorrect behavior.
4. A test where the search array length is not a multiple of VF, so the last search vector has inactive lanes. Ensure matches in inactive search lanes are ignored.
5. A test with multiple exits or outside uses to ensure the PHI node updates don't break LCSSA or dominance."
}

## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Prioritize Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Due to context length and round limit, you should prioritize test cases that are more relevant to the identified issues.

### Step 2: Understand the Test Case
- Analyze the selected test case to understand its structure, input characteristics, and what it is testing.
- Call the `verify` tool to see how the LLVM IR code in the test case is transformed by the optimization pass. This can help you understand the semantics of the test case and how it relates to the fix.

### Step 3: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues.

### Step 4: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 5: Analyze Results and Refine
- If the `verify` or `difftest` fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases. Check **Guidelines for Refining Test Cases** below for suggestions on how to refine test cases based on verification results.

### Step 6: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

Repeat the above steps for each test case until all test cases in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

### Important Rule
You can always keep mutating the test case and verifying it until you find an issue or are confident that no issues can be found. However, make sure it is aligned with the potential issues you identified in Phase 1. Meanwhile, avoid stucking on a test case for too long. If you have tried multiple mutations and still cannot find any issues, move on to other test cases or strategies.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

{
  "test_name": "find_first_of_i8",
  "test_body": "define ptr @find_first_of_i8(ptr %search_start, ptr %search_end, ptr %needle_start, ptr %needle_end) #0 {
entry:
  %search_test = icmp eq ptr %search_start, %search_end
  %needle_test = icmp eq ptr %needle_start, %needle_end
  %combined_test = or i1 %search_test, %needle_test
  br i1 %combined_test, label %exit, label %header

header:                                           ; preds = %search_check, %entry
  %search_ptr = phi ptr [ %search_next, %search_check ], [ %search_start, %entry ]
  %search_load = load i8, ptr %search_ptr, align 1
  br label %match_check

needle_check:                                     ; preds = %match_check
  %needle_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %needle_ptr, i64 1
  %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
  br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check

match_check:                                      ; preds = %needle_check, %header
  %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
  %needle_load = load i8, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
  %match_cmp = icmp eq i8 %search_load, %needle_load
  br i1 %match_cmp, label %exit, label %needle_check

search_check:                                     ; preds = %needle_check
  %search_next = getelementptr inbounds i8, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
  %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
  br i1 %search_cmp, label %exit, label %header

exit:                                             ; preds = %search_check, %match_check, %entry
  %res = phi ptr [ %search_end, %entry ], [ %search_ptr, %match_check ], [ %search_end, %search_check ]
  ret ptr %res
}

{"action":"test","test_index":4,"thoughts":"I will test the Match Accumulation Logic. The search array is "0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef" and the needle array is "xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx0". The first match is '0' at index 0 of the search array, which matches the last element of the needle array. The old code would have found '0' at index 0, but wait, the old code would check the first needle vector "xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx", which doesn't match anything. Then the second needle vector "xxxxxxxxxxxxxxx0" matches '0' at index 0. The old code would early exit when it finds a match, but since it iterates over needle vectors, it would find the match in the second needle vector. Wait, the old code early exits when ANY match is found in the current needle vector. So it would process the first needle vector, find no match, then process the second needle vector, find a match at index 0, and return index 0. This is actually correct.

entry:
  %dummy_val = getelementptr i8, ptr %search_start, i64 1
  br label %header

header:                                           ; preds = %search_check, %entry
  %search_ptr = phi ptr [ %search_next, %search_check ], [ %search_start, %entry ]
  %search_load = load i8, ptr %search_ptr, align 1
  br label %match_check

needle_check:                                     ; preds = %match_check
  %needle_next = getelementptr i8, ptr %needle_ptr, i64 1
  %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
  br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check

match_check:                                      ; preds = %needle_check, %header
  %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
  %needle_load = load i8, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
  %match_cmp = icmp eq i8 %search_load, %needle_load
  br i1 %match_cmp, label %found_match, label %needle_check

search_check:                                     ; preds = %needle_check
  %search_next = getelementptr i8, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
  %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
  br i1 %search_cmp, label %not_found, label %header

found_match:                                      ; preds = %match_check
  ret ptr %search_ptr

not_found:                                        ; preds = %search_check
  %unused = phi ptr [ %dummy_val, %search_check ]
  ret ptr null
}

{
  "test_name": "find_first_of_i16",
  "test_body": "define ptr @find_first_of_i16(ptr %search_start, ptr %search_end, ptr %needle_start, ptr %needle_end) #0 {
entry:
  %search_test = icmp eq ptr %search_start, %search_end
  %needle_test = icmp eq ptr %needle_start, %needle_end
  %combined_test = or i1 %search_test, %needle_test
  br i1 %combined_test, label %exit, label %header

header:                                           ; preds = %search_check, %entry
  %search_ptr = phi ptr [ %search_next, %search_check ], [ %search_start, %entry ]
  %search_load = load i16, ptr %search_ptr, align 1
  br label %match_check

needle_check:                                     ; preds = %match_check
  %needle_next = getelementptr inbounds i16, ptr %needle_ptr, i64 1
  %needle_cmp = icmp eq ptr %needle_next, %needle_end
  br i1 %needle_cmp, label %search_check, label %match_check

match_check:                                      ; preds = %needle_check, %header
  %needle_ptr = phi ptr [ %needle_start, %header ], [ %needle_next, %needle_check ]
  %needle_load = load i16, ptr %needle_ptr, align 1
  %match_cmp = icmp eq i16 %search_load, %needle_load
  br i1 %match_cmp, label %exit, label %needle_check

search_check:                                     ; preds = %needle_check
  %search_next = getelementptr inbounds i16, ptr %search_ptr, i64 1
  %search_cmp = icmp eq ptr %search_next, %search_end
  br i1 %search_cmp, label %exit, label %header

exit:                                             ; preds = %search_check, %match_check, %entry
  %res = phi ptr [ %search_end, %entry ], [ %search_ptr, %match_check ], [ %search_end, %search_check ]
  ret ptr %res
}
